parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I’ve been noticing lately that I hold my breath whenever a sudden noise happens, even when I know it’s completely harmless. It’s like my body automatically braces for impact before my brain even catches up. I catch myself doing it multiple times a day and just feel this weird jolt in my chest. It’s been going on for a few weeks now and it’s honestly just exhausting to constantly monitor my own breathing. I try to just ignore it, but the tension just lingers in my shoulders all day.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really draining. What you’re describing—your body reacting before your mind has a chance to reassure itself—can happen when your nervous system is stuck in a kind of “on guard” mode. Even if nothing is actually wrong, your body may be treating sudden sounds like a cue to brace.

A few things that might help in the moment:

- **Give the reaction less to “fight”**: when it happens, try noticing it with something like, “Ah, there’s the brace response,” rather than trying to force it away.
- **Lengthen the exhale**: a slow exhale can help your body settle. Even one or two longer breaths can make a difference.
- **Unclench your shoulders/jaw on purpose**: sometimes the breath and muscle tension are linked, so releasing one can help the other.
- **Check for patterns**: if it’s happening most when you’re tired, stressed, overstimulated, or in certain places, that can be useful information rather than a sign you’re doing anything wrong.

Since it’s been going on for a few weeks and feels exhausting, it may be worth paying attention to whether anything else changed around the same time—stress, sleep, caffeine, illness, or a specific event. If this is happening a lot or starting to affect your day, talking to a doctor or therapist could be a good idea, especially if it feels tied to anxiety or if you notice other symptoms like chest pain, dizziness, or shortness of breath.

If you want, I can also help you figure out a simple “in the moment” reset you can use when a noise triggers that brace response.

turns.user_message: That little habit has completely taken over my daily routine and I can’t seem to shake it off. Every single time the room gets quiet or my phone buzzes, my chest tightens up instantly. I find myself pacing around just to burn off this restless energy that builds up out of nowhere. It’s been weeks of feeling like I’m constantly waiting for something terrible to happen, even when everything is perfectly fine. I just feel so wound up all the time that my muscles actually ache from being so rigid.
turns.model_response:
That sounds exhausting — like your body is stuck on alert even when nothing is actually wrong. The constant tightness, pacing, and feeling like you’re waiting for something bad to happen can wear you down fast.

What you’re describing can happen when stress or anxiety gets “locked in” physically, but regardless of the cause, you deserve relief from it.

A few things that may help in the moment:
- **Slow your breathing down**: try inhaling for 4, exhaling for 6, for a couple of minutes.
- **Unclench your body on purpose**: drop your shoulders, loosen your jaw, unclasp your hands.
- **Give the restless energy somewhere to go**: a short walk, stretching, or even squeezing a pillow can help.
- **Reduce the “jolt” triggers** if you can: turn off nonessential notifications or set specific times to check your phone.
- **Name what’s happening**: “My body is on high alert right now, but I’m safe in this moment.”

If this has been going on for weeks and is affecting your day like this, it could be really worth talking to a mental health professional or a doctor. They can help figure out what’s fueling it and how to get you feeling steadier again.

If you want, I can also help you sort through what might be triggering it or walk you through a quick grounding exercise right now.

turns.user_message: The constant bracing has reached a point where I feel completely overwhelmed by my own nervous system. My heart starts pounding at the slightest shift in the atmosphere, and I can’t catch my breath no matter how hard I try. It feels like I’m trapped in this endless loop of anticipating disaster, and my whole body just refuses to stand down. I’m so exhausted from carrying this heavy weight around that even simple moments feel completely suffocating. Everything just feels too loud and too sharp, and I can’t find a single way to switch it off.
turns.model_response:
That sounds incredibly exhausting and scary. When your body is stuck in that constant “something’s about to go wrong” state, even ordinary moments can feel unbearable. I’m really sorry you’re carrying that much tension.

What you’re describing can happen when the nervous system has been pushed past its capacity for a while — it’s not you being weak or failing to cope. It’s your body acting like it’s in danger all the time.

A few things that may help a little in the moment:
- **Name what’s happening:** “My body is in alarm mode right now.” Sometimes that tiny bit of distance helps.
- **Lengthen the exhale:** Inhale gently for 4, exhale for 6–8, for a minute or two. Don’t force a deep breath if that makes it worse.
- **Orient to the room:** Look around and slowly name 5 things you can see, 4 you can feel, 3 you can hear.
- **Reduce input:** Dim lights, lower noise, get under a blanket, or hold something cool/warm.
- **Tiny physical signal of safety:** Feet on the floor, press your hands together, or lean against a wall.

If this feeling is happening a lot, it may really be worth reaching out to a therapist, doctor, or urgent care/mental health support — especially if the shortness of breath or pounding heart feels intense or new. You deserve support with this, not just brute-forcing your way through it.

If you want, I can stay with you and help you through a 60-second grounding exercise right now.
